title: Revisiting and updating molecular epidemiology of α-thalassemia mutations in Thailand using MLPA and new multiplex gap-PCR for nine α-thalassemia deletion.
author_string: Jomoui, Wittaya, Panyasai, Sitthichai, Sripornsawan, Pornpun, Tepakhan, Wanicha
abstract: α-thalassemia is an inherited blood disorder that is most frequently found in Southeast Asian populations. In Thailand, molecular characterization can diagnose most patients with α-thalassemia; however, several atypical patients are also observed in routine analyses. Here, we characterized α-thalassemia mutations among 137 Hemoglobin H (Hb H) disease patients and three fetuses of Hb Bart's hydrops, a fatal clinical phenotype of α-thalassemia. Specifically, we performed multiplex ligation-dependent probe amplification (MLPA) followed by direct DNA sequencing. We noticed common genotypes in 129 patients and eight patients had rare Hb H disease caused by compound heterozygous α<sup>0</sup>-thalassemia (--<sup>CR</sup> or --<sup>SA</sup> deletion) with α<sup>+</sup>-thalassemia (-α<sup>3.7</sup>/-α<sup>4.2</sup>/α<sup>Constant Spring</sup>α). Furthermore, two affected fetuses had the --<sup>SA</sup>/--<sup>SEA</sup> and one had the --<sup>CR</sup>/--<sup>SEA</sup> genotypes. Next, we developed and validated a new multiplex gap-PCR and applied this method to 844 subjects with microcytic red blood cells (RBCs) from various parts of Thailand. The frequency of heterozygous α<sup>0</sup>-thalassemia was dominated by --<sup>SEA</sup> 363/844 (43%), followed by --<sup>THAI</sup> 3/844 (0.4%), --<sup>SA</sup> 2/844 (0.2%), and --<sup>CR</sup> 2/844 (0.2%) mutations. These findings suggest that aforementioned four mutations should be routinely applied to increase the effectiveness of diagnosis and genetic counseling in this region.
